definition: Define a one-to-many dependency so when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified automatically.
ruleOfThumb: When many consumers react to one event source, publish updates instead of hard-coding each consumer call.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: What scalability problem is this notifier heading toward?

options.text: Every new reaction to order completion requires editing this method, coupling one event to many direct calls
options.explanation: Order completion has multiple downstream reactions. As more consumers appear, this method becomes a central switchboard instead of publishing one event.

options.text: The method should run analytics before email so event order remains deterministic
options.explanation: Call order may matter for some systems, but it is not the fundamental design scalability issue shown.

options.text: Using dot-chained service calls in one method is always a message-chain smell
options.explanation: These are separate collaborator calls, not chained traversal through internals. The issue is fan-out coupling.

options.text: The logic should be moved to a static helper for easier reuse
options.explanation: Static helpers do not decouple publisher and subscribers. They only move the same coupling elsewhere.

questions.prompt: A team wants to add Slack alerts and fraud scoring on successful payment. Which architecture insight matters most here?

options.text: Payment success should be published as an event so new listeners can be added without modifying PaymentService each time
options.explanation: Observer-style event publishing keeps the source stable while allowing independent subscribers for receipts, metrics, fraud, and alerts.

options.text: The service should call subscribers through a giant switch statement for better readability
options.explanation: A switch centralizes and grows modification points; it does not provide loose coupling.

options.text: The best fix is to combine receipts, metrics, and audit into one unified monitor class
options.explanation: Bundling concerns together increases coupling. These reactions typically evolve independently.

options.text: This code is already ideal; adding two more direct calls is the simplest and most maintainable option
options.explanation: Direct fan-out may be fine initially, but repeated growth creates brittle central logic and frequent edits.

questions.prompt: Which version better supports adding/removing side effects when user profiles are updated?

options.text: Version A — it hard-codes every reaction inside update(), so each new subscriber means reopening this service
options.explanation: Version A forces profile updates and all side effects to evolve together. Version B allows subscribers to change independently.

options.text: Version B — publishing events hides control flow and therefore is less maintainable than direct calls
options.explanation: Event-driven flow adds indirection, but here the tradeoff enables extension without constant source modification.
